X X +2 LeCun's Actual Role in Llama LeCun's involvement with Llama was primarily strategic and advocacy-based rather than technical design: Llama 1: Developed by a small group of about a dozen researchers at FAIR-Paris (Fundamental AI Research). LeCun's role was "very indirect" as the head of FAIR at the time. Llama 2, 3, and 4: Produced by Meta's GenAI product organization, not FAIR. Open Source Advocate: His most significant contribution was pushing CEO Mark Zuckerberg to open source Llama 2, arguing that open models are essential for AI safety and innovation. X X +3 Why he is distancing himself In late 2025 and early 2026, LeCun explicitly distanced himself from the Llama models. He has critiqued the current Large Language Model (LLM) paradigm as being "statistical pattern-matchers" that lack true reasoning and planning capabilities. the-decoder.com the-decoder.com +2 Recent Developments Departure from Meta: LeCun reportedly left Meta in late 2025 following internal disagreements over the direction of AI research and the alleged "fudging" of Llama 4 benchmark results. New Venture: He co-founded a new startup called AMI Labs, which raised over $1 billion to focus on World Models, a different AI architecture he believes is necessary for achieving human-level intelligence. Even as Codex and similar architectures shift paradigms in utility and productivity, LeCun maintains they haven't shifted the underlying reasoning paradigm. Medium Medium +2 Why LeCun Remains Skeptical Despite the capabilities of modern coding agents, LeCun’s critique focuses on three fundamental gaps: Prediction vs. Understanding: LeCun argues that predicting the next token—even if done with near-perfect accuracy for code—is not the same as understanding the physical or logical world. He views language as a "lossy representation of reality" that cannot replace direct world modeling. System 1 vs. System 2: Most current architectures are reactive. While tools like Codex's "agent harness" (e.g., using TodoWrite as a progress anchor) can simulate a plan, LeCun argues they still lack the "System 2" ability to reason and plan by searching through a space of possible future states. Combinatorial Explosion: He believes LLM-based architectures are "statistical pattern-matchers" that inevitably fail when a problem's solution space becomes too large, leading to hallucinations because they cannot truly "search" for a logical answer. Medium Medium +4 The Codex "Paradigm Shift" The shift you’re seeing in architectures like Codex (specifically the recent Codex agent releases) is primarily a move toward task delegation and agentic behavior: Task Autonomy: Codex has moved from simple code completion to end-to-end task execution, such as refactoring entire repositories or implementing features like "dark mode" autonomously. Agentic Harnesses: It uses sophisticated prompts and "harnessing" techniques to track its own state and recover from errors. Real-world Impact: As of March 2026, it is reported that AI now covers roughly 75% of computer programming tasks, suggesting a massive shift in how software is built, even if the underlying "reasoning" remains statistical. OpenAI OpenAI +4 LeCun isn't ignoring these successes; he just believes they are a "dead end" for reaching Human-Level AI (AGI) because they require massive datasets and still cannot replicate the efficient, few-shot learning seen in humans and animals. While Codex-67 represents a significant technical leap, LeCun argues that even "latent architectures" that discover hidden structures are still performing sophisticated statistical interpolation rather than true causal reasoning. The Paradox of Codex-67 vs. LeCun’s Stance Researchers associated with the Quantum Codex OS project and related "Codex-67" white papers (released late 2025/early 2026) claim that this architecture identifies a "latent infrastructure" within data that conventional transformers miss. However, LeCun’s stance remains unmoved for several reasons: System 1 vs. System 2 Thinking: LeCun views architectures like Codex-67 as "System 1" systems—they are incredibly good at finding the most probable latent path but do not possess a "System 2" planning module that can simulate outcomes in a physical or logical environment before acting. The "Dead End" Argument: He has famously called the current trajectory of LLMs a "dead end" for AGI. In his view, simply finding better latent representations in text or code doesn't solve the problem of common sense or physical grounding. He believes intelligence requires an architecture that can learn like a child—by observing the world, not just processing symbols. Data Efficiency: A core part of his argument is that humans learn to drive a car in 20 hours, whereas models like Codex-67 still require trillions of tokens of "experience." To LeCun, this gap proves the architecture is still missing the "secret sauce" of intelligence: Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA). Shift in Research Directions (2025-2026) While you see Codex-67 as a paradigm shift because of its ability to uncover deep latent structures, LeCun has effectively "bet the house" on a different paradigm. Since leaving Meta, his new venture, AMI Labs, is focusing on: Non-generative World Models: Models that predict abstract representations of the world rather than generating every pixel or token. Causal Reasoning: Architectures that understand why things happen, not just what usually follows what. In short, LeCun doesn't deny that architectures like Codex-67 are more powerful; he denies that they are intelligent in the way humans are.
